params.text:
What communication is

Communication is how we share information, thoughts and feelings with other people. Every time you speak, write, gesture, smile or simply listen, you are communicating. In care work it happens constantly — when you greet someone at the start of a shift, explain what you are about to do, or notice that a person seems quieter than usual.

Good communication is a two-way process. It is not only about giving information; it is about making sure the other person has understood, and that you have understood them. It involves words, but also tone of voice, expression and body language. When you get it right, the people you support feel heard, respected and safe.

Communication also flows in both directions at once. While you are speaking, the person is telling you things through their words, their face and their behaviour, and your task is to receive those messages as carefully as you send your own.

Why it is central to good care

Communication sits at the heart of everything you do. It is how you build trusting relationships, find out what someone wants, and give care that fits the individual rather than a routine. Poor communication is one of the most common causes of mistakes, complaints and distress, and it can leave people feeling ignored or unsafe.

  • Safety: clear handovers and records mean nothing important is missed.
  • Dignity: explaining and asking, rather than assuming, protects a person's rights.
  • Wellbeing: a friendly, patient manner reduces anxiety and loneliness.
  • Teamwork: sharing clear information helps colleagues and families work together.

Throughout this course you will build practical skills to communicate well with every person you support.
